summary:
Rembrandt van Rijn (1606–1669 AD) was the supreme artistic genius of the [[event:2326|Dutch Golden Age]] — a painter, draftsman, and printmaker whose mastery of light, shadow, and psychological depth produced works recognized as among the greatest in Western art history. Rembrandt's career embodied both the opportunities and precariousness of the Dutch commercial art market: unlike Italian Renaissance masters who worked under ecclesiastical or aristocratic patronage, Rembrandt worked primarily for Amsterdam's merchant class, producing portraits, biblical scenes, and group portraits (called 'corporation pieces') for a competitive market. His group portraits — particularly The Night Watch (1642) and The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp (1632) — captured the collective identity of Amsterdam's prosperous citizen guilds and civic organizations with unprecedented psychological complexity.

Rembrandt's technique — his mastery of chiaroscuro (contrasting light and shadow), his unprecedented series of self-portraits (over 40, creating an unparalleled record of aging and self-reflection), and his ability to convey psychological interiority — influenced painting for centuries. However, his personal life moved from remarkable success to financial ruin: his extravagant collecting habits and failed investments forced his bankruptcy in 1656, and he died poor in 1669. The Dutch Golden Age produced many other major artists — Vermeer's intimate domestic scenes, Hals's spontaneous portraiture, Jan Steen's comic genre scenes — constituting a flowering of visual art unmatched in Western history for its breadth and quality, made possible by the Dutch merchant class's willingness to commission and buy art for personal homes rather than only for churches and palaces.
